testcase: Clarification needed: PostResponseCodes rule conflict for LRO (TypeSpec vs lintdiff)
query:
title: Clarification needed: PostResponseCodes rule conflict for LRO (TypeSpec vs lintdiff)

question: I am seeing a validation conflict in [Swagger PR](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/28240) while implementing an LRO action (triggerRefresh) using the ArmResourceActionNoResponseContentAsync pattern 
 
Context:
Expected behavior is 202 (accepted) and 204 on completion (no response body)
We tried 202 + 204 + default which aligns with runtime behavior
However:
lintdiff/PostResponseCodes allows 202 + 204 + default
TypeSpec compile-time validation rejects 204
TypeSpec only allows:
202 + default (no body), OR
202 + 200 + default (with body)
Verified this on latest stable and also checked the rule implementation here: PostResponseCodes rule
Given this mismatch, had a few questions:
Are these failures potentially false positives?
Could this be a bug/inconsistency between lintdiff and TypeSpec validation?
Is 204 actually supported/recommended for completion of no-body LROs?
Or should we strictly use 202 + default (and avoid 204) going forward?
ground_truth:
This is a known issue with the swagger lintdiff rules for async POST operations: [[PostResponseCodes] LRO POST Responses checks are broken · Issue #809 · Azure/azure-openapi-validat…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-openapi-validator/issues/809)
 
In reality, the guidance is that for async POST operations the service should:
Only return a 202 or error, they should not return a 200 or 204 if the operation completes synchronously (the x-ms-long-tunning-operation-options show the response schema returned by the operationStatus endpoint in final-state-schema)
Represent their service APIs as they actually behave (meaning, if that operation doesn't return a 200 or 204, we should not model one
However, some older tools used to require a 'fake' 200 or 204 response to indicate the schema that was returned from oepratIonStatus when the operation completes.
 
To get your PR in while this bug is not fixed, you should do one of these two things:
Use ArmResourceActionNoResponseContentAsync or ActionAsync to model the operation correctly and suppress the lintdiff error (this will require s suppression review)
Model the operation using [ArmResourceActionAsync](https://azure.github.io/typespec-azure/playground/?options=%7B%22linterRuleSet%22%3A%7B%22extends%22%3A%5B%22%40azure-tools%2Ftypespec-azure-rulesets%2Fresource-manager%22%5D%7D%7D&%2BQAoWVudW3oARNzIHsKICDELjIwMjEtMTAtMDEtcHJldmlld8g1xDQgIOQA10NvbW1vblTkAY%2FHQCj1ATcuyykuyGoudjUpxEjHXscXCiAgYNJ7YCwKfeYAxUHoAMfrAOAg6AIC5ACabW9kZWwgRW1wbG95ZWUgaXMgVHJhY2tlZOgAlDzIHFByb3BlcnRpZXM%2B5QD2Li7pALjkAahQYXJhbWV0ZXLJMT476ACGyV9wyUTSfMpg6QFVQWdlIG9mIGXIP%2BUBSmFnZT86IGludDMyOwrHKUNpdHnSKmNpdHk%%2FaTHVwZGF0xE%2FFQ1XHDjogIsgLyjvpBHfpAMTmANxk5wGiU3VjY2VlZOUAxckM0z%2FFNuQBTWZhaWzJPkbFDTogIsYJ3Dh3YXMgY2FuY2XKPkPHD%2BQEz8cL%2FwFAIGRlbGXpAYBExA3mAPnICyLpA%2F3pA3flAS3qAcxib2R58gOA5QE55gIY7QC%2FdGFnc%2BcAri4uLvYEsUZvdW7kAXfkBKhBcm1UYWdz5wPPee0DMOkGfOQGkGlmaWPsBBJmb3IgdGhpc%2B4Cy%2BUDJMcjPzrvAK7HZWnkA5f3APX8BHPQRf8Eef8Eef8Eef8Eef8EefQA4m1vduoB1ekA3U1vdmVSxxXoANDkAXxtb3bkAj9mcm9tIGxvY%2BYCk%2BUAgMQT8QCuyzN0b88xdG%2FKL%2FcAlXNwb25z6wZi5gCWxxbsAJfuBUPFPsVkxnzmBMXNbmludGVyZuQHxU%2FoBWtzIGV4dGVuZHP2CIcuyyl7fQrlCGPII8oby1noAM3mAhFnZXTkA35ByjXkBb7sBucg5wRST3LnA0XOL0PHHVJlcGxhY2VBc3luY84%2F5wKsxjdDdXN0b21QYXRjaFPMKizvArjFOuYEDs9x5QQLZVdpdGhvdXRPa9NxbGlzdEJ5yDBHcm91cM9ETMUiUGFyZW501DxTdWJzY3JpcOUCFsY7xjPMGcw56ANbIHNhbXBs6wTaYWPFRHRoYXTmAgDpAx50byBkaWZmZeQAhO8CgyNzdXBwcmVzc%2F8K7e8K7S9hcm0tcG9zdC3pB6jEJOUCly3kA4VzIiAiTGludMR4IHJ1bGUgYnJva2VuIuYCk%2FABakHlALzuAWEs7AODLCB2b2lk8wD%2FSEVBROoILeQA62NoZWNr6gEXZXhpc3RlbuYJpyDGHkXJFO8CYs0d7gmC&e=%40azure-tools%2Ftypespec-autorest&vs=%7B%7D) and suppress the typespec linter rule

testcase: Need help in understanding why the ARMModelingReviewRequired label was added
query:
title: Need help in understanding why the ARMModelingReviewRequired label was added

question: Hi Team,
Our PR was added with ARMModelingReviewRequired label. We attended ARM office hrs and they are not clear on why is this being added. So want to understand why is this being added and what actions to be taken.
 
https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/42683
ground_truth:
The ARMModelingReviewRequired label is added automatically when the ARM Modeling Review GitHub Action cannot auto-sign off the management-plane modeling for your PR. In your case (PR #42683), the job ARM Modeling Review failed, so the workflow uploaded an artifact indicating ARMModelingReviewRequired and applied that label.
What this means:
Your RP is ARM management-plane, and the changes (new preview API version plus modeling changes like a new batch revoke path and new properties) require explicit ARM modeling review instead of auto-signoff.
Until modeling review is completed and marked as signed off (ARMModelingSignedOff / ARMSignedOff), the PR cannot complete the ARM step in the merge workflow.
Actions for you:
First resolve the Breaking Change(Cross‑Version) issues (the RemovedPath vs AddedPath and the new properties) according to the breaking change/versioning guidance. If those are intentional, follow the guidance at aka.ms/brch and the "Next Steps to Merge" comment in your PR (may involve requesting breaking change approval rather than changing the spec).
Once breaking change/Versioning issues are addressed and CI is re-run (/azp run as instructed), wait for the ARM modeling reviewer to look at the PR. The presence of ARMModelingReviewRequired simply puts you into the ARM modeling review queue.
If you need to unblock quickly or you don’t see movement after you have passed the breaking change step, use the ARM office hours per https://aka.ms/armofficehoursinfo or follow the "Getting help" / "Next Steps to Merge" guidance linked from the PR to contact the ARM reviewer on‑call.
You do not need to modify the ARMModelingReviewRequired label yourself; your task is to (a) fix/justify the breaking-change issues and (b) wait for or proactively engage ARM reviewers for modeling sign‑off.

testcase: How to add a new stable ApiVersion for Typespec
query:
title: How to add a new stable ApiVersion for Typespec

question: https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/28423 replace last preview apiVersion with new stable apiVersion, does it follow versiong policy of Typespec?
ground_truth:
Yes, the shape of your change is consistent with the TypeSpec + Azure versioning policy for management-plane APIs, assuming the service behavior matches the new spec.
You are doing two things:
Replacing the last preview API version 2025-06-01-preview with a new stable API version 2026-06-01.
Adding new read-only, optional properties in responses (supportChannel, chatConversationStatus).
Per the Azure/TypeSpec guidance:
When the last version is preview and you add a new stable version, you should:
Add a new stable version entry.
Keep only those previewed changes that are now considered stable and remove preview-only ones.
New properties that you keep must be backward compatible relative to the previous stable GA version, not relative to the preview.
Adding optional properties, including read-only ones, is a non-breaking (compatible) change.
So conceptually:
Comparing 2026-06-01 (stable) to 2025-06-01-preview is expected to show added optional/read-only properties; that’s allowed from the API design standpoint.
The oad rules (1040/1045) treat these as “breaking” relative to the previous preview for tooling reasons, but the correct resolution is not to remove the properties. Instead, you:
Keep those properties in the new stable TypeSpec model.
Ensure your TypeSpec source models this as a new version (e.g., @added(Versions."2026-06-01") on those properties if you are using TypeSpec versioning), and
Treat the oad check as expected noise and follow the documented breaking-change board process if needed.
In short: replacing the last preview with a new stable plus adding optional read-only response properties is aligned with TypeSpec/Azure versioning policy, as long as the new stable is backward compatible with the last stable version and your TypeSpec spec is versioned accordingly.

testcase: Question regarding typespec versioning
query:
title: Question regarding typespec versioning

question: I have a question regarding TypeSpec versioning discussion. During our TypeSpec version conversion, we were asked to maintain only one active preview version and one stable version for TypeSpec generation. But for my PR [Replace 2026-02-01 with 2026-02-01-preview for testing, iteration by chchen3 · Pull Request #28465 …](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/28465)  I got feedback on keeping both preview versions in TypeSpec generation. What is the current guideline? Should we keep all the preview versions from now on? 
```
[NEW]  Warning [TypeSpec versioning hygiene] main.tsp — line 52-57

The Versions enum was collapsed to a single v2026_02_01_preview entry. The previously shipped preview 2025-12-01-preview is no longer represented in TypeSpec, so its generated swagger (Microsoft.BillingBenefits/preview/2025-12-01-preview/billingbenefits.json) is now frozen and cannot be regenerated from this .tsp source. Spec history is no longer reproducible.

Classification: Introduced in this PR — the prior enum contained both v2025_12_01_preview and v2026_02_01.

Suggested fix: Restore v2025_12_01_preview: "2025-12-01-preview" to the Versions enum (without @Azure.Core.previewVersion since that marker applies only to the latest preview), and reintroduce @added(Versions.v2026_02_01_preview) on the properties/operations that genuinely first appear in 2026-02-01-preview. This preserves the ability to regenerate preview/2025-12-01-preview/ from source.
```
ground_truth:
For Azure Resource Manager TypeSpec specs the guideline has not changed: you should model your API with a single active preview version in TypeSpec at any point in time, plus all needed stable versions. You should not keep multiple previews active in the TypeSpec description.
Concretely:
In your enum Versions, the last value is the preview and is the only one that should have @previewVersion.
Older preview API versions should be removed from the TypeSpec Versions enum and from the versioning decorators, following the "single active preview" conversion guidance.
If rest-api-specs needs OpenAPI files for older previews (for retention/back‑compat checks), keep those OpenAPI files in the repo, but they do not need to come from the current TypeSpec generation. The perpetual-preview/how‑to docs describe how to split stable + new preview into separate PRs while still maintaining a single active preview in TypeSpec.
So for your PR: keep using the single‑active‑preview pattern in TypeSpec; if reviewers want older preview OpenAPI files retained, that should be handled at the OpenAPI layer, not by re‑introducing multiple preview versions into the TypeSpec model.
you can follow the detailed guidance here: [Adding a Preview Version when the Last Version was Preview | TypeSpec Azure](https://azure.github.io/typespec-azure/docs/howtos/versioning/02-preview-after-preview/)
If there is some need for the old preview swagger (e.g. if it's needed for RPaaS) there are instructions for keeping the swagger until it is officially retired referenced in the doc as well..

expected_references.title: Adding a Preview Version when the Last Version was Preview | TypeSpec Azure

testcase: New version with required status change
query:
title: New version with required status change

question: I have this PR [Add Bulkactions 2026-07-06-preview version by NneOkeke · Pull Request #43925 · Azure/azure-rest-api…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/43925) that makes a required field in v1 optional in a new version v2, but I get the breakingChange tag added, however when I read through the docs here  [openapi-diff/docs/rules/1025.md at master · Azure/openapi-diff](https://github.com/Azure/openapi-diff/blob/master/docs/rules/1025.md), it says making a required field optional in a new version isn't considered a breaking change, please help with this. Thanks!

```
1025 - RequiredStatusChange

Description: Checks whether an existing property's required status is changed from the previous specification.

Cause: This is considered a breaking change in the same api-version but not breaking in new api-version.

Example: required status for parameter b is changed from false to true without revising api-version.

Old specification
```
ground_truth:
i believe that rule is particularly about parameters for which it is not a breaking change. For properties returned by the api it is a breaking change, any users will now potentially get nothing instead

Another factor that might be complicating your PR.  The PR title says "add 2026-07-06-preview", but the file diff shows you are also modifying the swaggers for your existing API versions.  Some of these changes are only descriptions which should be fine, but others appear more structural, and could be causing problems.
 
You might be better off splitting this into two PRs.  One to update existing API versions (if needed), then another to add the new API version.

query:
title: Two TypeSpec projects under the same RP namespace — one with SDK, one without

question: We have two TypeSpec projects under `Microsoft.Monitor` for our SLI service, each with its own resource type registration:
- `Microsoft.Monitor.Slis.Management` — CRUD operations for the `slis` resource. SDK generation is needed.
- `Microsoft.Monitor.Slis.SignalPreview.Management` — A single resource provider level POST action (`sliSignalPreview`) used only via the Azure Portal. No SDK is needed for this.

Both share the same ARM provider namespace, and API version but live in separate folders under [monitor](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/tree/RPSaaSMaster/specification/monitor). They also share some common models which are currently duplicated.
Are there any concerns with this setup or would it be cleaner to house them together as a single service and suppress SignalPreview operation when SDK is being generated (is that even possible)?
ground_truth: You should talk with the SDK team before making any changes to the project structure.  Generally, an SDK is generated for a project, it is a bit complex to generate an SDK for only part of a project.

title: azure-rest-api-specs CI Swagger ModelValidation ：xxx is not a valid JSON Schema

question: This is my [PR](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/37995/checks). And I got many errors :　xxx  is not a valid JSON Schema　[link](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/actions/runs/18548447064/job/52870861336?pr=37995):
```

Error getting examples for specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/ChaosFaultGet.json: "/home/runner/work/azure-rest-api-specs/azure-rest-api-specs/specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/ChaosFaultGet.json" is not a valid JSON Schema
Error getting examples for specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/ChaosFaultList.json: "/home/runner/work/azure-rest-api-specs/azure-rest-api-specs/specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/ChaosFaultList.json" is not a valid JSON Schema
Error getting examples for specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/CosmosDBCassandraKeyspaceCreateUpdate.json: "/home/runner/work/azure-rest-api-specs/azure-rest-api-specs/specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/CosmosDBCassandraKeyspaceCreateUpdate.json" is not a valid JSON Schema
Error getting examples for specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/CosmosDBCassandraKeyspaceDelete.json: "/home/runner/work/azure-rest-api-specs/azure-rest-api-specs/specification/cosmos-db/CosmosDB.Management/examples/2025-05-01-preview/CosmosDBCassandraKeyspaceDelete.json" is not a valid JSON Schema
...
```
 
And I am sure that these are valid  JSON. 
Besides, the latest merged [PR](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/35785) did not have the same problem.
 
Thanks for you help！
ground_truth:
The errors you saw in ModelValidation appear to be related to example values that the tool claims are not valid UUIDs or ARM IDs, which you should verify. There was also mention of a missing example and potential file name capitalization issues, but those were not confirmed in your case.
One issue with your PR was that LintDiff crashed due to an out-of-memory error, which was fixed by increasing max-old-space-size from 8GB to 16GB.
Regarding the repeated “is not a valid JSON Schema” messages, this seems to be a harmless bug in the new oav-runner and spec-model code. The problem is triggered because the spec has nested folders under examples. ModelValidation historically only validated examples in the top-level examples folder and ignored nested ones.
Going forward, we need to decide whether to support nested examples or block them. Since nested examples already exist in main, banning them would be difficult. The current plan is to update ModelValidation to handle nested examples.

ground_truth:
The Go SDK validation is failing because the TypeSpec validation pipeline enforces that all required emitter options for Go are present and correctly formatted in your tspconfig.yaml, even if you are not actively generating Go SDKs. This is a standard requirement for all Azure management-plane TypeSpec projects in the azure-rest-api-specs repository.
In your PR, the Go emitter configuration in tspconfig.yaml uses placeholder values (e.g., service-dir: sdk/resourcemanager/placeholder, module: github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-go/{service-dir}/armplaceholder). These placeholder values are intentionally added by the Azure SDK team to pass validation, but they are not meant for actual SDK generation. The validation step checks for the presence and format of these options, not whether you intend to generate the SDK.
If you do not plan to generate a Go SDK, the recommended approach is to suppress the Go-specific validation sub-rules in your TypeSpec validation suppression file. This is an accepted practice for services that do not support a Go SDK. Here is an example suppression entry you can add to your suppressions.yaml (or similar suppression file):
```
- tool: TypeSpecValidation
  paths:
    - tspconfig.yaml
  rules:
    - SdkTspConfigValidation
  sub-rules:
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.generate-fakes
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.inject-spans
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.service-dir
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.package-dir
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.module
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.fix-const-stuttering
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.generate-samples
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.head-as-boolean
    - options.@azure-tools/typespec-go.inject-spans
  reason: 'This service does not support a Go SDK currently.'
```
This will bypass the Go SDK validation errors and allow your PR to pass the required checks. For more details, see the Azure TypeSpec Validation documentation on suppressing sub-rules.
If you ever plan to support Go SDK generation in the future, you should update the tspconfig.yaml with the correct values as per the [official guidance](https://aka.ms/azsdk/spec-gen-sdk-config).

1. About the AvoidAdditionalProperties suppression
PR comment - https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/27476#discussion_r2990080483.
Have been having a chat with the bot about this in the API Spec Review channel - Shaarad Dalvi: AvoidAdditionalProperties suppression for parameters of policy init... | Azure SDK > API Spec Review | Microsoft Teams.
The assignmentParameters are the parameters for Azure Policy. On the GitHub PR comment discussion I have shared the link of the TypeSpec of policy where the type is mentioned as unknown, since the parameter could be an array, string, Boolean, number or an object.
Justification for the suppression - Our RP is not acting on the parameter values directly. At the time of our Sovereign View resource creation, our RP tries to send these parameters to policy to create the assignments. If the policy assignment fails due to parameter mismatch, the appropriate error would be returned to the user and the Sovereign View resource won't be created.
 
2. About the usage of ArmResourceDeleteWithoutOkAsync
Copilot PR Comment - https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/27476#discussion_r2974613569
We wish to start with an implementation of synchronous delete and soon (in a few weeks) wish to move to async delete. My understanding is that usage of ArmResourceDeleteWithoutOkAsync gives support for synchronous delete (by returning 204 No Content) as well as asynchronous delete (by returning 202 Accepted). Also, if as per the Copilot comment if I use ArmResourceDeleteAsync, TypeSpec linter shows deperecation warning. So -
a. Is ArmResourceDeleteWithoutOkAsync enough for our sync as well as async support for deletion?
b. Is there any need to migrate to possibly deprecated ArmResourceDeleteAsync?
 
3. Usage of uuid[] as per Copilot suggestion for subscription Ids
Copilot PR comment - https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/pull/27476#discussion_r3019650035
We have subscription Ids in our resource properties for which the copilot is suggesting to use uuid[]. However, this fails the LintDiff check [Add Sovereign View resource type to Microsoft.Sovereign · Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr@47ba812](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs-pr/actions/runs/24082456991/job/70246338044?pr=27476) and tells 'Usage of Guid is not recommended. If GUIDs are absolutely required in your service, please get sign off from the Azure API review board.' Should I keep these as string[] instead?
 
Any help on these questions would be much appreciated!
ground_truth:
It is not the case that ArmResourceDeleteWithoutOKAsync (or any async delete) supports synchronous delete.  The 204 response occurs if a resource is already deleted or deleting at the time of the request.  204 is not returned synchronously after a successful delete operation.
 
While your API is in preview and not visible to customers, it is acceptable to make breaking changes like making synchronous operations asynchronous.  After your API is available to customers, breaking changes are discouraged, and generally require a breaking change review, especially once the api-version is stable.

On the other questions,  unknown types are discouraged because they require the user to have external knowledge of how to make a successful request.  If you're not in control of the schemas for policy, then you likely don't have much choice, and this suppression would probably be approved by ARM.
 
I don't see the uuid violation, but this violation is likely outdated.  The latest common-types use uuid to describe subscription ids: [azure-rest-api-specs/specification/common-types/resource-management/v6/types.json at main · Azure/a…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/main/specification/common-types/resource-management/v6/types.json#L663-L670)
 
Any consumer of the spec should be able to handle this type, as this is just a format over the string type, so any emitter without a specific uuid type would still correctly treat these as strings.  Meanwhile, you are providing extra documentation and validation.

testcase: Allow property update only through PUT and not Patch
query:
title: Allow property update only through PUT and not Patch

question: Hi Team,
 
Our resource properties contain some optional fields that can be created with some value and later updated to null if customer wants and we support both PUT and PATCH calls the the resource. However RPaaS behavior will not allow to remove an existing field from resource through Patch ([rpaas - How to can customers and user RP remove properties during PATCH with - Stack Overflow at Mi…](https://stackoverflow.microsoft.com/questions/442814/442831#442831)), it looks inconsistent if a customer tries to patch it to null. With UpdateProperties auto generated from TSP, is there any suggestion how to not allow these be updated through Patch. We will set the expectation that it has to be updated through a PUT call.
ground_truth:
Here are you talking about the envelope properties, or the rt-specific property bag of your resource?  ARM RPC strongly suggests that Json MergePatch semantics are followed for the rt-specific proeprty bag, and seems that RPaaS needs to support this.

For envelope properties, which generally come from common types, there is an effort to specify PATCH behavior for common-types that would include envelope properties, but this is unlikely to be concluded until the new year..
 
There is no really good way to specify properties that are unerasable in PATCH, although there is a proposal to use defaults to allow this ( default value would be the value the property is set to when a 'null' is sent on the wire).
 
For now, the best advice is to ensure that patch request properties are optional and (for now) have no defaults.  It is inadvisable to exclude properties settable by the user from PATCH requests.
